justification: Chapter 3 contains a dedicated section 'Holy Mary, Mother of God, Perpetual Virgin, Queen of Heaven' (lines 640–689) that argues Mary carries Hellenistic queen-mother imagery drawn from Isis, Ptolemaic queens, and divine motherhood traditions — including 'Mary as Queen of Heaven,' 'Mary as New Eve,' and perpetual virginity as dynastic-political rhetoric. The Artemis-Mary continuity argument (pagan goddess absorbed into Christian Marian theology via shared iconography of priestesses/nuns, Second Eve role, and salvific motherhood) is new evidence that directly reinforces this existing argument. Chapter 6 has no Mary content anywhere.

Chapter 6 already explicitly discusses Theos Hypsistos dedications at lines 151–156 within 'The Eastern Mediterranean Synthesis' section, as evidence of the shared civilizational grammar and pre-Christian monotheistic infrastructure across Asia Minor, the Levant, and Cyprus. The finding adds documentary depth to that existing argument: the Theos Hypsistos cult thriving specifically in Corinth, Athens, and Macedonia, with 'god-fearers' as the identifiable conversion pipeline into Christianity, directly supports chapter 6's thesis that Christianity grew out of Greco-institutional foundations rather than emerging from a vacuum. Chapter 3's single reference to 'Son of the Most High' as a royal title is a different argument about Christological titles, not about the institutional conversion infrastructure this finding describes.
